1:
"And the second lot came forth to Simeon, even for the tribe of the children of Simeon according to their families: and their inheritance was within the inheritance of the children of Judah." The witnesses note the second lot falling to Simeon, whose inheritance lay within Judah's portion. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the second of the seven lots, Benjamin's being the first. The witnesses note that Henry notes Simeon's lot was drawn after Judah's, within Judah's inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the second lot — to Simeon; the within Judah — no separate land; the Jacob's prophecy — scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7); the discipline with mercy — kept within God's people.

The witnesses agree Simeon's portion fell "within the inheritance of the children of Judah," a circumstance full of meaning: Jacob had prophesied of Simeon (and Levi), "I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel" (Gen. 49:7), for their cruelty at Shechem; that word was now fulfilled, Simeon given no separate, compact land but cities scattered amid Judah's; yet the chastisement was tempered with mercy, for Simeon was lodged near the royal tribe of Christ; it teaches that God's ancient words are surely fulfilled, his discipline upon sin real yet mingled with grace, the scattered tribe still kept within the inheritance of his people, "I will... scatter them" yet not cast them off, Simeon's lot within Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Simeon within Judah: The witnesses note the the second lot: to Simeon. The witnesses note the within Judah: no separate land. The witnesses note the Jacob's prophecy: scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7). The witnesses note the discipline with mercy: kept within God's people. The expositors observe the the second lot: to Simeon. The expositors observe the within Judah: no separate land. The expositors observe the Jacob's prophecy: scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7). The expositors observe the discipline with mercy: kept within God's people. The believer is taught to mark the the second lot, the within Judah, the Jacob's prophecy, the discipline with mercy, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Simeon's lot fell within Judah's inheritance — Jacob's prophecy of scattering fulfilled, God's discipline real yet mingled with mercy, the scattered tribe kept within his people (Gen. 49:7).
2:
"And they had in their inheritance Beersheba, and Sheba, and Moladah," The witnesses note the cities of Simeon's inheritance, beginning with Beersheba. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beersheba and Sheba, the particle sometimes used so, lest the count exceed the thirteen cities given. The witnesses note that Moladah was among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Beersheba — the well of the oath; the Sheba — and Moladah; the the patriarchs' wells — hallowed of old; the the covenant remembered — he hath remembered his covenant for ever (Ps. 105:8-9).

The witnesses agree Simeon's cities open with Beersheba, the storied well-town of the patriarchs, where Abraham and Isaac dug wells and called on the name of the LORD (Gen. 21:33): the southernmost city, "Beersheba" — the well of the oath — now fell to Simeon; the place hallowed by the covenant-dealings of the fathers became part of the heritage; it teaches that God settles his people in places where his name was called upon of old, the well of the oath a token of his sworn faithfulness through the generations, "he hath remembered his covenant for ever... which he made with Abraham" (Ps. 105:8-9), Beersheba among Simeon's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Beersheba the well of the oath: The witnesses note the Beersheba: the well of the oath. The witnesses note the Sheba: and Moladah. The witnesses note the the patriarchs' wells: hallowed of old. The witnesses note the the covenant remembered: he hath remembered his covenant for ever (Ps. 105:8-9). The expositors observe the Beersheba: the well of the oath. The expositors observe the Sheba: and Moladah. The expositors observe the the patriarchs' wells: hallowed of old. The expositors observe the the covenant remembered: he hath remembered his covenant for ever (Ps. 105:8-9). The verse thus records that Simeon's cities began with Beersheba, the patriarchs' well of the oath — God settling his people where his name was called of old, the well a token of his sworn faithfulness, who has remembered his covenant for ever (Ps. 105:8-9).
3:

The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Hazar-shual, Balah, and Azem among the cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Hazar-shual — the jackal's village; the Balah — and Azem; the the dry south — the Negeb; the a portion in the wild — he satisfied the longing soul (Ps. 107:4-9).

The witnesses agree the listing of Simeon's towns continues — "Hazarshual, and Balah, and Azem": these were cities in the dry south, the Negeb, a hard and thirsty country, yet given to Simeon for a heritage; the name "Hazar-shual" means "the village of the jackal," a token of the wild and lonely region; the granting of even such land shows that God gives his people a portion in every kind of place, the hard and barren as well as the rich; it teaches that the LORD's heritage reaches even into the dry places, and that his people may dwell in the wilderness yet under his care, "they wandered in the wilderness... yet he... satisfied the longing soul" (Ps. 107:4-9), Hazar-shual among Simeon's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities of the south: The witnesses note the Hazar-shual: the jackal's village. The witnesses note the Balah: and Azem. The witnesses note the the dry south: the Negeb. The witnesses note the a portion in the wild: he satisfied the longing soul (Ps. 107:4-9). The expositors observe the a portion in the wild: he satisfied the longing soul (Ps. 107:4-9). 4:
"And Eltolad, and Bethul, and Hormah," The witnesses note Eltolad, Bethul, and Hormah among Simeon's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were all cities of Judah; Eltolad the same with Tolad, Bethul with Bethuel. The witnesses note that Hormah is named with them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Eltolad — and Bethul; the Hormah — of solemn memory; the defeat in presumption — victory in obedience; the nothing without him — without me ye can do nothing (John 15:5).

The witnesses agree the list names Hormah, a place of solemn memory: there Israel, presuming to go up against the Canaanites without God after their unbelief, was beaten down (Num. 14:45), and there later, by God's help, the Canaanite was utterly destroyed and the place named Hormah, "devotion" or "destruction" (Num. 21:3); the city of both Israel's defeat in presumption and their victory in obedience now fell to Simeon; it teaches that the scenes of God's dealings — judgment on self-will and triumph through faith — are bound up in his people's heritage, a standing lesson that "without me ye can do nothing" (John 15:5), Hormah among Simeon's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Hormah of defeat and victory: The witnesses note the Eltolad: and Bethul. The witnesses note the Hormah: of solemn memory. The witnesses note the defeat in presumption: victory in obedience. The witnesses note the nothing without him: without me ye can do nothing (John 15:5). The expositors observe the Eltolad: and Bethul. The expositors observe the Hormah: of solemn memory. The expositors observe the defeat in presumption: victory in obedience. The expositors observe the nothing without him: without me ye can do nothing (John 15:5). 5:
"And Ziklag, and Bethmarcaboth, and Hazarsusah," The witnesses note Ziklag, the city later given to David, among Simeon's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Ziklag, another of the cities of Judah. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Beth-marcaboth, "the house of chariots," likely where war-chariots were kept. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Ziklag — david's refuge; the Beth-marcaboth — the house of chariots; the burned and recovered — by Amalek; the faith in distress — encouraged himself in the LORD (1 Sam. 30:6).

The witnesses agree the list names Ziklag, a town of great note in David's history: when fleeing from Saul, David received Ziklag from the Philistine king Achish, and there he dwelt and was made strong (1 Sam. 27:6); there too the Amalekites burned the city and took the wives, and David "encouraged himself in the LORD his God" and recovered all (1 Sam. 30:6); the city of Simeon's heritage became a refuge and proving-ground of the future king; it teaches that God provides his servants a place of shelter in their distress, and that faith, even in deepest loss, strengthens itself in the LORD, "encouraged himself in the LORD his God," Ziklag among Simeon's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Ziklag of David: The witnesses note the Ziklag: David's refuge. The witnesses note the Beth-marcaboth: the house of chariots. The witnesses note the burned and recovered: by Amalek. The witnesses note the faith in distress: encouraged himself in the LORD (1 Sam. 30:6). The expositors observe the Ziklag: David's refuge. The expositors observe the Beth-marcaboth: the house of chariots. The expositors observe the burned and recovered: by Amalek. 6:
"And Bethlebaoth, and Sharuhen; thirteen cities and their villages:" The witnesses note the close of Simeon's first group — thirteen cities with their villages. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beth-lebaoth, perhaps the same with Lebaoth, a city of Judah; some say an idol-temple to lions was here. The witnesses note that the group numbered thirteen cities with their villages. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Beth-lebaoth — the house of lionesses; the Sharuhen — among them; the thirteen cities — exactly counted; the the den cleansed — they shall not hurt in all my holy mountain (Isa. 11:6-9).

The witnesses agree the first group of Simeon's towns closes with "thirteen cities and their villages": Gill notes a tradition that "Beth-lebaoth" — the house of lionesses — had been a heathen temple dedicated to lions; the place of beasts and false worship now stood in the heritage of God's people, to be cleansed and possessed; the exact count of thirteen marks the completeness of the record; it teaches that God reckons his gifts precisely and turns even the dens of idolatry into the dwellings of his own, the house of lions become a city of Simeon, "the wolf also shall dwell with the lamb... they shall not hurt nor destroy in all my holy mountain" (Isa. 11:6-9), thirteen cities given to Simeon.

So the witnesses make the verse thirteen cities: The witnesses note the Beth-lebaoth: the house of lionesses. The witnesses note the Sharuhen: among them. The witnesses note the thirteen cities: exactly counted. The witnesses note the the den cleansed: they shall not hurt in all my holy mountain (Isa. 11:6-9). The expositors observe the Beth-lebaoth: the house of lionesses. The expositors observe the Sharuhen: among them. The expositors observe the thirteen cities: exactly counted. The expositors observe the the den cleansed: they shall not hurt in all my holy mountain (Isa. 11:6-9). 7:

The witnesses note that Gill notes Ain and Remmon were cities of Judah. The witnesses note that the group numbered four cities with their villages. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Ain — a spring; the Remmon — a pomegranate; the Ether and Ashan — the four cities; the water in the desert — i will make the wilderness a pool (Isa. 41:18).

The witnesses agree a second group of Simeon's towns is named — "Ain, Remmon, and Ether, and Ashan; four cities": "Ain" means a spring or fountain, and "Remmon" a pomegranate, names that breathe of water and fruitfulness in the dry south; even in the parched Negeb, Simeon's heritage held springs and gardens; it teaches that God provides refreshment and fruit for his people even in hard places, the fountain and the pomegranate in the wilderness a token of his bounty, "I will open rivers in high places, and fountains in the midst of the valleys: I will make the wilderness a pool of water" (Isa. 41:18), the four cities of Simeon's second group.

So the witnesses make the verse the fountain and the pomegranate: The witnesses note the Ain: a spring. The witnesses note the Remmon: a pomegranate. 8:

The witnesses note the villages round about completing Simeon's inheritance, to Baalath-beer in the south. The witnesses agree Simeon's portion is rounded off with "all the villages... round about these cities... to Baalathbeer, Ramath of the south": the heritage reached to the far south, taking in not only the named cities but the scattered hamlets between; the full account marks the completeness of even this scattered tribe's portion; it teaches that God leaves none of his people without a place, gathering even the small and scattered into a sure inheritance, the least tribe provided to the last village, "he gathereth together the outcasts of Israel" (Ps. 147:2), Simeon's inheritance complete to the southern bound.

9:
"Out of the portion of the children of Judah was the inheritance of the children of Simeon: for the part of the children of Judah was too much for them: therefore the children of Simeon had their inheritance within the inheritance of them." The witnesses note that Simeon's portion was taken from Judah's, which was too large for Judah alone. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is why no border of Simeon is described, it being within Judah's, already given. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Simeon's inheritance taken from Judah's too-large portion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the out of Judah's portion — simeon provided; the Judah's part too much — larger than needed; the within their inheritance — the greater sharing; the brotherly supply — your abundance a supply for their want (2 Cor. 8:14).

The witnesses agree the reason for Simeon's lodging within Judah is here given — "the part of the children of Judah was too much for them": Judah's lot, first drawn, proved larger than the tribe needed, and so a portion was yielded to Simeon; the greater tribe shared its abundance with the lesser, the strong making room for the weak; it teaches the law of brotherly love and mutual provision among God's people, that those who have abundance supply the want of others, "your abundance may be a supply for their want... that there may be equality" (2 Cor. 8:14), Simeon provided out of Judah's overplus.

So the witnesses make the verse out of Judah's portion: The witnesses note the out of Judah's portion: Simeon provided. The witnesses note the Judah's part too much: larger than needed. 10:

The witnesses agree the third lot came up for Zebulun, its bound beginning at Sarid: the tribe of Zebulun, one of the sons of Leah, now received its place in the north of the land; the orderly progress of the lots, each in its turn, shows God's impartial dealing, every tribe brought to its portion; it teaches that the LORD remembers all his people in their order, none passed by, each in due season receiving the inheritance appointed, "he shall choose our inheritance for us, the excellency of Jacob whom he loved" (Ps. 47:4), the third lot come up for Zebulun.

11:
"And their border went up toward the sea, and Maralah, and reached to Dabbasheth, and reached to the river that is before Jokneam;" The witnesses note Zebulun's border going toward the sea, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the border went westward toward the Mediterranean, fulfilling the prophecies of Jacob and Moses that Zebulun should dwell by the sea and be a haven of ships. The witnesses note that it reached to the river before Jokneam. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the toward the sea — the border went; the a haven of ships — jacob's word (Gen. 49:13); the the abundance of the seas — moses' word (Deut. 33:19); the the promise sure — not one word hath failed (1 Kgs 8:56).

The witnesses agree Zebulun's border "went up toward the sea," fulfilling to the letter the dying word of Jacob: "Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea; and he shall be for an haven of ships" (Gen. 49:13), and Moses' word that he should "suck of the abundance of the seas" (Deut. 33:19); the placing of the tribe by the sea, centuries after the prophecy, proves the certainty of God's word; it teaches that not one word of the LORD's promises falls to the ground, the inheritance answering exactly to the ancient blessing, "there hath not failed one word of all his good promise" (1 Kgs 8:56), Zebulun set by the sea as Jacob foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse Zebulun by the sea: The witnesses note the toward the sea: the border went. The witnesses note the a haven of ships: Jacob's word (Gen. 49:13). The witnesses note the the abundance of the seas: Moses' word (Deut. 33:19). The witnesses note the the promise sure: not one word hath failed (1 Kgs 8:56). 12:
"And turned from Sarid eastward toward the sunrising unto the border of Chislothtabor, and then goeth out to Daberath, and goeth up to Japhia," The witnesses note Zebulun's southern border running eastward by Chisloth-tabor toward Mount Tabor. The witnesses note that Gill notes this describes the southern border, going from west to east. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the border eastward to Chisloth-tabor and Daberath. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eastward — toward the sunrising; the Chisloth-tabor — near Mount Tabor; the Daberath and Japhia — the eastern bound; the borders set by God — thou hast set all the borders of the earth (Ps. 74:17).

The witnesses agree Zebulun's border ran eastward "toward the sunrising... unto the border of Chislothtabor," near the great Mount Tabor: the tribe's land lay in the region of Galilee, by the famous mountain where in later days great things would be done; the careful tracing of the border shows the exactness of the inheritance, fixed by God's appointment from sea to mountain; it teaches that the LORD's gift to his people is bounded with precision, the land marked from the western sea to the eastern heights, each tribe set in its appointed compass, "thou hast set all the borders of the earth" (Ps. 74:17), Zebulun's border by Tabor.

13:
"And from thence passeth on along on the east to Gittahhepher, to Ittahkazin, and goeth out to Remmonmethoar to Neah;" The witnesses note Zebulun's border passing Gittah-hepher, the native town of the prophet Jonah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Gittah-hepher — gath-hepher; the Jonah the prophet — born there; the the sign of Jonah — christ's resurrection (Matt. 12:40); the a prophet from Galilee — refuting the sneer (John 7:52).

The witnesses agree the border passed "Gittahhepher" — Gath-hepher, the birthplace of Jonah the prophet (2 Kgs 14:25): the town of Zebulun's heritage gave to Israel the prophet whom the Lord sent to Nineveh, and whose three days in the fish's belly Christ made the great sign of his own death and resurrection (Matt. 12:40); the city in Galilee bred a prophet, refuting the later sneer that "out of Galilee ariseth no prophet" (John 7:52); it teaches that God raises up his messengers where he will, and that Jonah's sign points to the risen Christ, Gath-hepher the home of Jonah on Zebulun's border.

14:
